steps.title: Identify what the blank connects
steps.content:
The blank sits between the title phrase "little-known French portrait artist" and the proper name "Marie-Denise Villers." The name is a restrictive appositive — it uniquely identifies which artist — so no punctuation should separate it from the title. Eliminate any choice that inserts punctuation.
  • Choice A — 'artist—': An em dash before the name implies a sharp break or non-essential aside. No such break is warranted here. ✗
  • Choice B — 'artist': No punctuation between the title and the name. The name flows directly as the essential identifier of the artist. ✓
  • Choice C — 'artist:': A colon introduces lists or explanations. There is no list or formal explanation following — just a proper name. ✗
  • Choice D — 'artist,': A comma before the name suggests it is non-restrictive (optional). Because the name is essential, this comma is incorrect. ✗

steps.title: Apply the restrictive appositive rule
steps.content: When a proper name uniquely identifies the preceding noun — as "Marie-Denise Villers" identifies "artist" here — no punctuation separates them in Standard English. Every other option inserts punctuation where none belongs. That matches choice B — B is correct.
